coredata.dc:description: The escalating number of patients affected by various diseases, such as gout, attributed to abnormal uric acid (UA) concentrations in body fluids, has underscored the need for rapid, efficient, highly sensitive, and stable UA detection methods and sensors. Optical sensors have garnered significant attention due to their simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and resistance to electromagnetic interference. Notably, research efforts have been directed towards UA on-site detection, enabling daily monitoring at home and facilitating rapid disease screening in the community. This review aims to systematically categorize and provide detailed descriptions of the notable achievements and emerging technologies in UA optical sensors over the past five years. The review highlights the advantages of each sensor while also identifying their limitations in on-site applications. Furthermore, recent progress in instrumentation and the application of UA on-site detection in body fluids is discussed, along with the existing challenges and prospects for future development. The review serves as an informative resource, offering technical insights and promising directions for future research in the design and application of on-site optical sensors for UA detection.

head.ce:title: Progress in optical sensors-based uric acid detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The escalating number of patients affected by various diseases, such as gout, attributed to abnormal uric acid (UA) concentrations in body fluids, has underscored the need for rapid, efficient, highly sensitive, and stable UA detection methods and sensors. Optical sensors have garnered significant attention due to their simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and resistance to electromagnetic interference. Notably, research efforts have been directed towards UA on-site detection, enabling daily monitoring at home and facilitating rapid disease screening in the community. This review aims to systematically categorize and provide detailed descriptions of the notable achievements and emerging technologies in UA optical sensors over the past five years. The review highlights the advantages of each sensor while also identifying their limitations in on-site applications. Furthermore, recent progress in instrumentation and the application of UA on-site detection in body fluids is discussed, along with the existing challenges and prospects for future development. The review serves as an informative resource, offering technical insights and promising directions for future research in the design and application of on-site optical sensors for UA detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para:
Uric acid (UA) is the final product of purine metabolism (Maiuolo et al., 2016), characterized by its white crystal appearance. UA exhibits slight solubility in water (Wang and Königsberger, 1998) and remains poorly soluble in human body fluids, predominantly as urates, with concentrations of 0.13–0.46 mM (2.18–7.7 mg/dL) in serum and 1.49–4.46 mM (25–74 mg/dL) in urine (Azmi et al., 2015). Renal metabolism eliminates UA from the body through urine (El Ridi and Tallima, 2017). In certain organisms such as birds, reptiles, and primates, UA can be oxidized to allantoin by the action of uricase, along with the generation of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), as depicted in reaction (1) (Sarma et al., 1999). However, this enzymatic process is hindered in humans due to the absence of uricase (Kratzer et al., 2014). Consequently, when UA levels rise, the excess UA cannot be decomposed and instead accumulates in joints, tendons, and surrounding tissues, primarily in the extremities, leading to the development of gout, a form of arthritis (MCCARTY and HOLLANDER, 1961; Merriman and Dalbeth, 2011). Furthermore, elevated levels of UA have been associated with various conditions, including kidney stones (Grassi et al., 2013), hypertension (Bainbridge and Roberts, 2008), metabolic syndrome (Ford et al., 2007; Kanbay et al., 2016), chronic kidney disease (Johnson et al., 2013), and cardiovascular diseases (Feig et al., 2008; Saito et al., 2021). Conversely, a low concentration of UA has been linked to multiple sclerosis (Moccia et al., 2015) and certain genetic diseases (Corey-Bloom et al., 2020) (Fig. 1 ). Therefore, the detection of UA has always been a crucial area of focus for researchers in the fields of healthcare and disease diagnosis.
(1) UA + H2O + O2 → allantoin + CO2 + H2O2
In the early stages, the phosphotungstic method was widely employed for UA determination (Henry et al., 1957; Pileggi et al., 1972). As researchers delved further into UA and analytical tools developed, numerous methods emerged for UA detection, including optical techniques (Li et al., 2019a; Lin et al., 2022; Tripathi and Chung, 2022; Yaacob et al., 2022), electrochemistry (Kim et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Pleskacova et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2013), gas chromatography (Li et al., 2018), capillary electrophoresis (Zhao et al., 2008), among others. However, the latter three methods are complex, time-consuming, and reliant on bulky instrumentation, limiting their widespread application. Electrochemical sensors, in contrast, exhibit high sensitivity, rapid response, and seamless integration into flexible wearable devices (Fang et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021a). Nonetheless, they are susceptible to electrode surface contamination or corrosion, entail high processing costs for certain sensor fabrication processes, display sensitivity to temperature, humidity, pH variations, and are prone to environmental electromagnetic interference. Conversely, optical sensors are easily fabricated, convenient to operate, cost-effective, observable, and impervious to electromagnetic interference, rendering them suitable for deployment in complex environments (Fauzi et al., 2021; Gerdan et al., 2022). Presently, diverse optical sensors have been developed for quantitative UA analysis, encompassing colorimetry (Badoei-Dalfard et al., 2019; Qin et al., 2021), fluorescence (Kumari et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019c), chemiluminescence (Saqib et al., 2018), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Alula et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2022), and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) (Jain et al., 2020). Correspondingly, microplate readers, spectrophotometers, fluorescence spectrometers, Raman spectrometers, and SPR instruments are commonly employed for UA detection (Pal and Pal, 2016; Schrenkhammer and Wolfbeis, 2008; Singh et al., 2019). In these methods, the UA concentration is solely correlated with optical signals, such as color change, fluorescence production and quenching, as well as changes in spectral patterns and peak shifts. Due to their immunity to electromagnetic interference, optical sensing systems demonstrate stable performance with a comparatively high signal-to-noise ratio even in complex environments.

The estimated prevalence of gout in Western societies has been reported to range from 2.7% to 6.7% (Loganathan et al., 2022). Gout patients often require hospitalization, leading to high costs, labor-intensive procedures, and inconveniences. Additionally, elderly individuals residing in care facilities often face suboptimal living conditions (Nguyen et al., 2022). Consequently, there is an urgent and growing need for home-based treatment and monitoring. However, on-site UA detection has been constrained by sampling methods and testing instruments. Conventional serum UA tests inflict significant discomfort on patients and increase the risk of blood-related infections and wound complications, making them unsuitable for routine UA monitoring. Moreover, traditional testing instruments such as commercial microplate readers, spectrometers, and microscopes are bulky and intricate, posing challenges for widespread adoption in community and home settings. Recently, alternative samples such as urine, sweat, saliva, tears, and interstitial fluids have emerged for non-invasive UA testing due to their general linear correlation with serum UA levels (Bilancio et al., 2019; Park et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2022e; Zheng et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021b). This expansion in UA sample collection methods has opened up new possibilities for portable, particularly wearable, sensing and non-invasive testing. Furthermore, researchers are increasingly focusing on the development of portable instruments that can be utilized on-site, including smartphones, small specialized devices, and wearable technologies (Ahmad et al., 2021; Manjakkal et al., 2021; Meng et al. 2022a, 2022b). Optical sensors, particularly enzyme-free sensors, have significantly enhanced adaptability to various environments and exhibited high immunity to interference, offering unparalleled advantages for on-site UA detection.

In recent years, several reviews have been published on the topic of UA detection (Aafria et al., 2022; Hernández-Ramírez et al., 2021; Nangare et al., 2021; Ratautaite et al., 2021; Tukimin et al., 2018; Vernerova et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020). However, most of these reviews have primarily focused on electrochemical approaches, lacking comprehensive coverage of optical sensing systems and on-site applications. Nevertheless, optical sensors used for UA detection have garnered considerable research attention, resulting in rapid advancements in reducing detection limits, expanding the linear range, improving sensitivity, and integrating with on-site instruments for body fluid testing. Hence, it is imperative to present and consolidate these recent breakthroughs while carefully examining the challenges they encounter in relation to enzymatic dependency, selectivity, automation, and wearability. In this review, we specifically focus on optical sensors for UA detection, highlighting the latest research advances from the past five years. These advancements are systematically described and classified in detail. Among the various optical sensor types, colorimetric and fluorescent sensors emerge as the most commonly employed approaches. For colorimetric sensors, we innovatively divide them into two categories: 3,3′,5,5′- tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) color development-based sensors and Ag/Au nanomaterials-based sensors. The TMB color development-based approaches are further subcategorized into forward oxidation and inverse reduction processes, which correspond to uricase-based and uricase-free methods, respectively. Fluorescent sensors are divided into single fluorescence-based and ratiometric fluorescence-based sensors based on the number of emission peaks. Optical supramolecular-based sensors are presented as a distinct class of fluorescent sensors due to their unique principles. Additionally, chemiluminescence-based sensors, SERS-based sensors, and SPR-based sensors are also commonly applied optical sensors in quantitative UA analysis. We have summarized and compared the sensors discussed in this study, elucidating their unique strengths and limitations, with a specific focus on their applicability in on-site and body fluid detection scenarios. Moreover, we have identified and examined the key challenges encountered by UA optical sensors, and have proposed potential strategies to overcome these challenges. Furthermore, we have delved into the exploration of future trends and prospective advancements within this field, paving the way for further research and innovation.

The colorimetric method is primarily based on Lambert-Beer’s law (Mäntele and Deniz, 2017), which utilizes optical devices such as spectrophotometers to establish a correlation between the concentration of the analyte and the absorbance of the solution. In certain cases, qualitative analysis can be conducted visually. However, more advanced and intelligent applications can be achieved in other cases using the RGB values of photos captured by smartphones and the spectra detected by spectrometers (Fig. 2 A). In this section, our focus has been on sensors based on the color development of TMB and sensors based on Ag/Au nanomaterials, which represent the majority of colorimetric UA detection studies. Furthermore, we have categorized the TMB color development-based sensing approach into the forward oxidation and inverse reduction processes to provide a clearer understanding of the relationship between these studies.

In the colorimetric assays for UA detection, the conventional and widely adopted approach is the color rendering system based on TMB, H2O2, and horseradish peroxidase (HRP). Upon the presence of H2O2 generated from UA oxidation through urease, TMB undergoes catalytic oxidation, resulting in the formation of oxTMB and a noticeable change in solution color from colorless to blue (Zhang et al., 2020b). This process establishes a linear relationship between the concentration of UA and the absorbance of the solution (Fig. 2B). Building upon this principle, Wang et al., (2018b) developed a multilayer-modified paper-based colorimetric sensing platform for UA detection, offering the advantages of convenience and low cost. Similar research has also been reported by Ornelas-González et al. (Ornelas-González et al., (2022).
However, the high cost, limited stability, and environmental restrictions associated with HRP have motivated researchers to explore alternative nano enzymes as substitutes for natural enzymes.

In some studies, metal-organic frameworks (MOF) have exhibited peroxidase-like activity. Badoei-dalfard et al. (Badoei-Dalfard et al., 2019) designed a thorium-organic framework (Th-MOF) as an artificial peroxidase using ultrasound-assisted reverse micelle (UARM) techniques. They proposed a colorimetric approach (Th-MOF/uricase) utilizing H2O2 and TMB, which enabled quantitative detection of UA owing to the peroxidase-like activity of Th-MOF. Chi et al., (2022) introduced a copper nano zyme consisting of a copper phosphate framework and organic components. Notably, they embedded uricase within the copper nano zyme, merging the two oxidation steps of UA and TMB into a single-step reaction. Quantum dots have also been widely employed as peroxide-mimetic enzymes. Liang et al., (2020) utilized Passiflora edulis Sims to synthesize carbon quantum dots co-doped with iron and nitrogen (Fe@NCDs) through a one-pot hydrothermal method. These Fe@NCDs demonstrated remarkable peroxidase-mimetic activity, exhibiting cost-effectiveness and excellent stability. In the realm of nano enzymes, metal nanoparticles have garnered significant attention. Li et al., (2021) developed chitosan-stabilized Au NPs as peroxidase mimics, facilitating the oxidation of TMB and enabling colorimetric detection of UA. Similarly, 1-H-3-methylimidazolium acetate-capped nickel nanoparticles (Ni NPs) have exhibited inherent peroxidase-like properties, as demonstrated in the study by Nishan et al., (2022).

Although these studies have demonstrated exemplary performance in developing alternative HRP-mimicking enzymes, the linear range of UA in these studies is significantly inadequate to encompass the concentration range of UA in human body fluids. To expand the linear range, inspired by the catalytic center of HRP, Ma et al., (2021) developed an efficient peptide-based HRP mimic (CCA-YH) which had a similar catalytic center structure of HRP through the covalent co-assembly strategy. Compared to natural HRP and other mimetic peroxidases, CCA-YH exhibited enhanced catalytic activity, superior stability, and reusability across various environmental conditions such as pH and temperature. The colorimetric method based on CCA-YH demonstrated a broader linear detection range of UA from 5 to 800 μM, with a lower LOD of 0.577 μM. Prussian blue (PB), another artificial peroxidase with good activity, can also trigger the color reaction of TMB in the presence of H2O2. However, its inherent color interference limits its application in optical sensors. To overcome this challenge, Niu et al., (2018) proposed a common carbon cloth growing with PB to fabricate a peroxidase mimic without background interference. Similarly, Li et al., (2022c) immobilized active PB on a flexible Au fiber, which exhibited excellent peroxidase-like activity (Fig. 2D). The obtained carbon cloth-based and Au fiber-based nano zyme can be easily introduced and removed from the reaction systems, providing well-controlled reaction processes and improved material utilization. Moreover, these approaches offer new possibilities for wearable sensors, leveraging the flexibility of carriers for nano enzymes. This opens up opportunities for non-invasive UA detection and personalized health monitoring.

The incorporation of mimetic enzymes has significantly reduced the reliance on natural enzymes in colorimetric sensing systems, demonstrating promising performance. However, the presence of uricase, which is responsible for the specific oxidation of UA, remains a limitation due to its undesirable stability, repeatability, and storability, hindering the on-site application of the colorimetric sensors. To address this issue and establish a completely enzyme-free colorimetric sensing system, an inverse reduction process based on the TMB development system was proposed (Fig. 2C). Unlike the conventional forward oxidation process, where UA is oxidized to generate H2O2, the inverse reaction involves direct addition of H2O2 and nano enzymes to the reaction system, catalytically oxidizing TMB. The presence of UA inhibits this reaction due to its reducing property. As a result, a colorimetric enzyme-free sensing system for UA is established.

He et al., (2018) developed a monolithic peroxidase mimic by designing CoP nanosheet arrays grown on Ni foam. This CoP/NF nano zyme exhibited consistent activity across a wide pH range of 2–14 and temperatures ranging from 5 to 90 °C, surpassing the performance of natural enzymes. The oxidation products, oxTMB, could be selectively reduced to TMB by UA, resulting in the solution lightning in blue. In addition to biomimetic enzymes, Wang et al., (2018a) proposed a novel method for UA quantitative detection without the use of natural or biomimetic enzymes. This approach relied on the redox reaction between chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) and UA. In the presence of UA, Au3+ ions were reduced to other valence states, inhibiting the oxidation of TMB to oxTMB by HAuCl4. This inhibition was manifested as a decrease in the absorption peak at 652 nm, allowing for sensitive detection of UA with a low LOD of 0.67 nM. To further streamline the sensing system, Yang et al., (2022c) integrated Fe3O4 NPs into MnO2 NS to construct Fe3O4@MnO2 NS as a high-efficiency artificial enzyme, which could catalytically oxidize TMB without the need for H2O2. Upon the addition of UA to the solution, MnO2 NS was decomposed into Mn2+, resulting in the corresponding lightning of the solution color. This color change could be conveniently measured using an ultraviolet spectrophotometer or a smartphone. Furthermore, smartphone-captured images could be converted into RGB values and analyzed for the quantification of UA with a linear range of 1–70 μM.

In comparison to the conventional approach based on the forward oxidation process of TMB, the enzyme-free inverse process offers superior resistance to environmental conditions and enhanced stability of the sensing system. However, it is important to address the challenge of maintaining the selectivity of UA detection in the presence of certain antioxidants found in human body fluids, such as ascorbic acid, cysteine, melatonin, and glutathione. These antioxidants have the potential to inhibit the oxidation process of TMB, thus affecting the specificity of the assay. The reaction mechanisms, the involvement of uricase and HRP, as well as the synthesis methods employed for the various mimic enzymes in the mentioned studies in this section, are all summarized in Table SI.

Noble metal nanomaterials, particularly silver (Ag) and gold (Au), have been extensively employed in the quantitative analysis of UA, exhibiting various morphological characteristics such as nanoparticles (NPs) (Misra et al., 2013), nanostars (Dandu et al., 2023), and nanorods (Kannan et al., 2013). These materials are favored for their strong localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) peak in the visible region. The size, morphology, distance, and dielectric environment of Ag/Au nanomaterials can undergo modifications under the influence of the external environment, leading to changes in LSPR peak intensity, wavelength shift, and color variations in the solution. These alterations can be precisely captured and quantified using optical instruments such as spectrophotometers.

Wang et al., (2018c) described an enzyme-free colorimetric sensor for UA detection utilizing silver triangular nanoprisms (Ag TNPs). Through the etching of Ag TNPs along the (1 1 0) facets with H2O2, the original triangular shape transformed into round nanodiscs, causing a color change in the solution from navy blue to light blue along with a decrease in absorbance. In the presence of UA, the (1 1 0) facets of Ag TNPs became adhered to by UA, preventing further etching. Consequently, the solution regained its navy blue color, and the absorbance increased. The increase in absorbance at 683 nm of Ag TNPs exhibited a linear relationship with the UA concentration within the range of 0.1–45 μM. Similarly, Li et al., (2020a) reported a colorimetric method for UA detection based on Ag NPs and HAuCl4. The oxidation of Ag atoms to Ag + by HAuCl4 led to the reduction of Au3+ to Au atoms, which then deposited on the surface of Ag NPs, forming honeycomb Au–Ag NPs and causing a color change from yellow to brown. Upon the addition of UA, this process was inhibited, resulting in a lighter solution color and a linear increase in absorbance with UA concentration. Apart from Ag nanomaterials, Au nanoparticles are widely employed in UA sensing. In the work of Rezaei et al., (2022), the generation of I2 through the reaction of I− and Cu2+ facilitated the oxidation of Au atoms to AuI, which settled on the surface of Au NPs, leading to a decrease in the absorbance peak. UA preferentially reacted with I2 due to its stronger reducing properties, thereby safeguarding the surface of Au NPs from further deposition.

These studies have established a relationship between absorbance values and UA concentrations, providing a practical approach. However, deviations from Lambert-Beer's law will occur when the concentration of an absorbing substance exceeds 0.01 M or when the concentration of specific large ions or molecules falls below 10 μM (Østergaard, 2016). Moreover, absorbance values below 0.1 will result in a concomitant reduction in sensor sensitivity, rendering it challenging to distinguish subtle changes in analyte concentrations. Therefore, some research has adopted the utilization of wavelength shift (Δλ) as an alternative to absorbance values for quantitative analysis of UA concentration.

Amjadi et al., (2020) proposed a colorimetric sensor for UA detection based on Ag+ and Au nanorods (Au NRs). The reduction of Ag+ ions to Ag atoms by UA led to the formation of Ag/Au core-shell NRs, resulting in a blue shift of the LSPR peak of Au NRs at 740 nm. The extent of this blue shift was found to be directly proportional to the UA concentration. Despite the low LOD of 0.065 μM, the narrow linear range of 0.1–1.0 μM necessitates significant sample dilution when used for detecting UA in real urine or serum samples. This extensive dilution has the potential to introduce inaccuracies in the detection results. Qin et al., (2021) proposed another approach based on wavelength shift for UA detection by employing etched Au NRs (Fig. 2E). Au NRs were etched by I2, resulting in a reduction in aspect ratio from 5:1 to 2:1, causing a blue shift of the characteristic LSPR absorbance peak of Au NRs from 965 nm to 695 nm. In the presence of UA, the redox process responsible for I2 production was inhibited, leading to a reduced blueshift. By measuring the wavelength shift (Δλ) rather than the value of absorbance, the potential inaccuracies associated with absorbance measurement were minimized. Nevertheless, it is important to note that most of these studies have primarily relied on UA’s reducing properties, thereby facing a similar challenge encountered by TMB-based sensors: the presence of various reducing substances in body fluids significantly interferes with the selectivity of UA. In addition, further research is needed to address the repeatability and interference resistance of Ag/Au nanomaterials in these sensing systems.

Colorimetric sensors, encompassing TMB color development-based sensors and Ag/Au nanomaterials-based sensors, have emerged as popular options for UA sensing. Under favorable environmental conditions, the introduction of uricase can selectively oxidize UA, significantly enhancing the specificity of UA detection. Moreover, within the TMB-based sensing system, the integration of metal ions like Ag+, Au3+, and Cr6+ as sensing receptors, along with a sensing array, effectively enables the discrimination of UA from other highly reducing substances, thereby elevating the selectivity of UA detection (Zhang et al., 2020a). Furthermore, researchers can broaden their exploration beyond Au and Ag to investigate the robust interactions between UA and other metal ions, including chromium, manganese, iron, zinc, and copper (Masoud et al., 2012; Moawad, 2002; Qu et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020). These metal ions exhibit the ability to form complexes with UA, offering a foundation for the design of optical sensors. This alternative approach surpasses sole reliance on the reducing properties of UA, opening new avenues for advanced sensing techniques. In terms of detection instrumentation, the conspicuous color changes observed enable direct identification by the naked eye and accurate quantification using devices such as smartphones and spectrometers. However, the availability of portable spectrophotometric instruments remains relatively limited, and the quantification of UA is predominantly accomplished using large microplate readers. This observation highlights the tremendous potential for future advancements in this field, particularly in the domains of wearable sensing and home medical monitoring. The nanomaterials, the synthesis methods, and the measurement methods with the wavelength in the mentioned studies in this section are all summarized in Table SII.

Some chemicals have the ability to absorb and store energy when exposed to the excitation light, causing them to enter an excited state. Upon returning to the ground state, the excess energy can be emitted as electromagnetic radiation, resulting in fluorescence. Various mechanisms, including energy transfer, electron transfer, charge diverting, and surface effects, can lead to the quenching of fluorescence (Fig. 3 A) (De Silva et al., 2009; Suzuki and Yokoyama, 2015; Yang et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2022a), forming the basis of fluorescence sensors. In this section, the fluorescence methods are categorized into two groups based on the number of emission peaks: single fluorescence-based sensors and ratiometric fluorescence-based sensors (Fig. 3B). Optical supramolecular-based sensors are presented separately due to the unique principles.

Kumari et al., (2018) synthesized napthyridine-rhodamine based probes with a strong affinity for the detection of low concentrations of UA, demonstrating a LOD of 12 nM owing to their robust hydrogen bonding interaction. Upon the addition of UA, the color of the napthyridine-rhodamine probe solution underwent a noticeable change to pink, accompanied by the emergence of a new peak at 587 nm with increasing intensity in fluorescence spectroscopy. Due to its non-toxic nature, the fluorescence probe has been applied for in vitro detection of UA in living HeLa cells. Nevertheless, the synthesis of probes and reagents is a complex process. In contrast, the synthesis of quantum dots is generally more straightforward.

Quantum dots have garnered significant interest among researchers owing to their notable advantages, including excellent stability, high fluorescence intensity, and high sensitivity. Pang et al., (2022) synthesized ascorbic acid (AA)-derived carbon dots (A-CDs) via one-step hydrothermal synthesis which exhibited green fluorescence when excited by a 420 nm laser (Fig. 3C). The fluorescence could be linearly quenched by UA in the range of 0–56 μM. However, the sensing system can be easily disrupted by carbohydrates that are rich in –OH groups and substances with strong reducing properties. To further minimize the interference of substances on the selectivity of UA, Peng et al., (2022) prepared silicon quantum dots/Cu-modified-β-cyclodextrin nanoclusters via coordination-driven self-assembly between Cu
2
-β-CD and –NH2 on the surface of SiQDs. This sensing system effectively eliminates interference from various reducing substances, including AA, but is, unfortunately, still subject to interference from DA. Similarly, Zhang et al., (2022c) synthesized Ag NPs wrapped with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) as a fluorescence probe. This study successfully eliminated interference from most interfering substances, with the exception of AA. Wang et al., (2022c) prepared nitrogen-enriched carbon dots (NCDs) by self-polymerization method. The green fluorescence generated by NCDs could hardly be quenched by 27 interfering substances and ions, including AA, DA, and GSH. The sensing system exhibits robust anti-interference capabilities.

In addition to quantum dots, MOFs have gained considerable attention in fluorescence-based detection methods in recent years. Han et al., (2020) synthesized a europium-based metal−organic framework (Eu2(TDA)4(OOCCH3)2(H2O)2, referred to as Eu-TDA) as a fluorescent probe for UA detection. The absorption peaks of UA and Eu-TDA partially overlapped, leading to a competition in energy absorption when light passed through the sample. This resulted in UA filtering the light absorption of Eu-TDA and subsequently quenching its luminescence. As a result, the luminescence of Eu-TDA decayed with the increase in UA concentration. Among numerous metal ions and anions, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, MnO4 −, Cr2O7 2−, and CrO4 2− exhibited significant quenching effects on the fluorescence intensity of Eu-TDA. Yang et al., (2022a) developed a Cu (II) functionalized MOF (Cu2+@Tb-MOFs) for UA detection. In contrast to previous methods relying on the quenching effect of UA on fluorescent probes, this study demonstrated a unique phenomenon. The presence of UA facilitated the removal of Cu2+ ions from the surface of Cu2+@Tb-MOFs, leading to the recovery of fluorescence. As a result of the strong interaction between Cu2+ and UA, the sensing system exhibited an exceptional capability to detect UA over an extensive concentration range of 0–104 μM, surpassing the limitations of previous fluorescence-based methods. Meanwhile, even after the addition of other biological metabolites, the fluorescence of the composite material did not recover, demonstrating the superior anti-interference capability of the sensing system. To further eliminate the influence of interferences, Shatery et al. (Shatery and Omer, 2022) combined quantum dots with MOF and encapsulated blue emissive carbon dots (bCDs) into the pores of chromium-based MOFs (Cr-MOFs) for the first time which has a stronger anti-interference capability than bCDs alone (Fig. 3D).

Conventional single fluorescent probes have demonstrated good performance in UA detection. However, their susceptibility to interfering substances, including dopamine and ascorbic acid, restricts their applicability in complex human body fluids. Meanwhile, environmental conditions such as temperature and background light can easily interfere with detection results. To address these challenges and enhance the stability and accuracy of sensing systems, the integration of ratiometric fluorescent probes has been introduced. By employing two distinct emission peaks, these probes enable self-calibration and normalization of environmental variations, resulting in more reliable and accurate detection results.

In recent studies employing ratiometric fluorescence, the oxidation product of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) known as oxOPD has been widely utilized as the fluorescent quenching agent (Ye et al., 2020). Liu et al., (2020) synthesized glutathione functionalized Ti3C2 MQDs (GSH-Ti3C2 MQDs) with a fluorescence emission at 430 nm. Upon the generation of H2O2 through the oxidation of UA by uricase, OPD was oxidized to oxOPD by HRP with a fluorescence emission at 568 nm. Simultaneously, the emission at 430 nm of GSH-Ti3C2 MQDs experienced progressively quenched by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between GSH-Ti3C2 MQDs and oxOPD. This approach enabled the determination of UA through the ratiometric fluorescence method, utilizing the fluorescence intensity ratio (F568/F430) for more accurate measurements. In another study, Rong et al., (2022) synthesized a novel heparin sulfur quantum dots (Hep-SQDs) using heparin as a passivator (Fig. 3E). OPD was oxidized to oxOPD with a fluorescence emission at 560 nm, while the fluorescence emission of Hep-SQDs at 467 nm was quenched by oxOPD. A ratiometric fluorescence approach (F560/F467) was established for UA detection. HRP was also used in the catalytic oxidation of OPD in this work. The similar principle has been employed in the work of Wang et al., (2019c).

In these studies, the use of HRP as a catalyst for the oxidation of OPD to oxOPD is common practice. However, natural enzymes have certain limitations that restrict their application. These limitations include sensitivity to environmental conditions and inherent drawbacks. In order to overcome these challenges and reduce dependence on enzymes, researchers have started to investigate the potential of mimetic peroxidases as alternative catalysts.

In a typical study conducted by Xiao et al., (2018), they proposed a B, N-carbon dots-based method and synthesized ZnFe2O4 magnetic microspheres as mimetic peroxidase which can also catalyze the oxidation of OPD in the presence of H2O2 (Fig. 3F). The fluorescence emission of B, N-CDs at 430 nm could be quenched by oxOPD through the inner filter effect, enabling the establishment of a fluorescence intensity ratio (I556/I430). Additionally, the presence of oxOPD resulted in a distinct yellow color, allowing the quantification of UA through colorimetric methods that detected the absorption peak at 420 nm. This research has effectively reduced reliance on natural enzymes, thereby expanding the utility of the fluorescence sensor. Compared to simple colorimetric sensor, this sensing system integrated both colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence techniques, facilitating more accurate and reliable detection of UA. Similarly, Wu et al., (2019) synthesized a Si NPs-uricase-I−-based UA probe using a similar approach of combining colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence principles. Iodide was employed as a mimic peroxidase, and the fluorophore Si NPs, with a fluorescence peak at 450 nm, exhibited quenching in the presence of oxOPD. The change in fluorescence intensity ratio (I565/I450) was utilized to determine UA concentration.

Recently, researchers have made advancements in the design of nanomaterials possessing both fluorescent and peroxidase-like properties, resulting in simplified sensing systems. Li et al., (2022a) prepared iron, cobalt, and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (FeCo/N-CDs) with the peroxidase-like activity, enabling the catalysis of OPD oxidation. Simultaneously, the fluorescence emitted by FeCo/N-CDs at 475 nm could be quenched by oxOPD. Zhang et al., (2022d) also designed iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Fe/N-CDs) possessing these dual properties. The functional CDs synthesized exhibited a Michaelis constant (Km) value of 1.297 mM with H2O2. Moreover, this dual-functional nanomaterial has the capability to reduce reagent consumption in sensing systems. Regrettably, all of these methods require the involvement of uricase, indicating that they are not entirely enzyme-free systems and may be subject to limitations imposed by environmental conditions during usage.

In addition to the ratiometric fluorescence methods based on OPD, Yue et al., (2019) proposed a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence approach utilizing N, Fe-doped carbon dots (N, Fe-CDs) and bovine serum albumin (BSA)-stabilized silver nanoclusters (BSA-Ag NCs). H2O2 produced from the oxidation of UA by uricase could be catalyzed to generate hydroxyl radicals by N, Fe-CDs. These hydroxyl radicals, in turn, etched BSA-Ag NCs, resulting in decreased fluorescence at 650 nm. Concurrently, the fluorescence at 450 nm of N, Fe-CDs exhibited an increase. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I450/I650) was employed to quantify UA concentrations, demonstrating a linear range of 0.8–133 μM and a LOD of 0.49 μM. This sensing method without OPD still relied on the presence of urease and was susceptible to interference from glutathione (GSH) and cysteine (Cys).

The ratiometric fluorescence method has gained popularity due to its ability to establish a relationship between UA concentration and the ratio of two fluorescence intensities, thereby offering improved resistance to interference and enhanced system stability (Rong et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2020). Unlike single fluorescence-based methods which primarily rely on the reducing properties of UA to quench fluorescence, ratiometric fluorescence-based methods often incorporate uricase to enhance selectivity for UA (Liu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2022d). However, H2O2 generated during the oxidation process can take a redox reaction with AA, thereby interfering with UA selectivity. Additionally, the GSH and Cys can also inhibit the oxidation of OPD to oxOPD. Moreover, the stringent temperature requirements associated with enzyme activity further impede the on-site application of these sensors. These challenges represent common issues encountered in ratiometric fluorescence-based approaches for UA detection in human body fluids.

Supramolecular chemistry has emerged as a prominent interdisciplinary field in recent years, characterized by its simplicity and environmentally friendly nature (Albrecht, 2007; Ariga et al., 2013). In particular, the indicator displacement assay (IDA) has garnered significant attention as a classical method for constructing fluorescent probes (Sedgwick et al., 2021). IDA offers several advantages, including high sensitivity, the ease of constructing, and establishing sensing arrays, making it a popular choice for fluorescence sensing applications.

Zhang et al., (2022f) proposed an optical supramolecular sensing system that offers the capability to convert microscopic amounts of UA molecules into macroscopic fluorescence intensity. In this study, Guanidinocalix[5]arene (GC5A) was screened to bind with UA strongly and selectively, effectively replacing fluorescein (Fl) with UA. The fluorescence intensity of the excited Fl exhibited a linear increase within the concentration range of 0–47.64 μM of UA. This sensing approach demonstrates superior selectivity for UA and is less susceptible to interference from other substances compared to fluorescence methods utilizing QDs, MOF, and OPD. The unique and robust binding properties of UA to supramolecular macrocycles contribute to the enhanced selectivity. Furthermore, this sensor has been successfully employed in differentiating patients with hyperuricemia from normal volunteers using both real and artificial urine samples, laying the foundation for on-site and home monitoring of UA.

In summary, fluorescence assays generally have higher sensitivity and lower LOD than absorbance assays. However, the high sensitivity to UA also leads to a generally narrower detection range, rendering these sensors unable to directly determine high concentrations of UA in human body fluids. Since fluorescence quenching relies solely on the reducing nature of UA, the fluorescence intensity of the sensing system cannot establish a specific correlation exclusively with UA concentration. There will also be competing reactions from other reducing interfering substances. Furthermore, despite the complex synthesis process, operational procedures, and reliance on enzymes, there are several studies that hold immense potential in the realm of community medicine and family healthcare. The reaction mechanism, involvement of uricase, FL probe, synthesis methods, excitation wavelength, and emission wavelength in the mentioned studies in this section are all summarized in Table SIII.

Chemiluminescence is a phenomenon of light radiation that occurs during a chemical reaction of substances without the need for external light excitation (Li et al., 2014; Song et al., 2019; Tiwari and Dhoble, 2018; Yang et al., 2020a). It can be classified into direct chemiluminescence and indirect chemiluminescence (Fig. 4 A). In direct chemiluminescence, substances A and B undergo a chemical reaction to produce substance C, which absorbs the energy released from the reaction and transitions to an excited state, C*. As C* returns to the ground state, it emits light radiation. On the other hand, indirect chemiluminescence, also known as energy transfer chemiluminescence, occurs when reactants A and B react to generate an excited state intermediate, D* (energy donor). Upon decomposition of D*, it releases energy that is transferred to E (energy acceptor), causing E to become excited and transition to an excited state, E*. Finally, when E* returns to the ground state, light emission occurs. Chemiluminescence is widely employed in various fields, including UA detection, due to its high sensitivity, specificity, wide linear range, and simplicity.

Saqib et al., (2018) presented a luminol-NHPI chemiluminescence system based on the reagent N-hydroxyphthalimide (NHPI), where NHPI was utilized as a coreactant with luminol. This system demonstrated a remarkable ability to detect UA by accurately quenching the intense chemiluminescence generated by the NHPI-luminol reaction. The linear range for UA detection extended from 50 to 8000 pM, with an exceptional LOD as low as 10 pM, making it at least four orders of magnitude more sensitive than previous analytical methods. However, it should be noted that the CL intensity was almost completely quenched in the presence of 100 μM UA, which is below the lower limit of the UA concentration range typically found in normal human serum. AA interfered with the sensing system, although not significantly. Nevertheless, this method offers a promising concept for the future development of efficient and high-sensitive UA detection methods. In a similar vein, Huang et al., (2022) constructed an enzyme-free chemiluminescence sensor for UA detection. They synthesized bimetallic CuCo Prussian blue analogue (CuCo PBA) nanocubes with enzyme-like properties that induced chemiluminescence of luminol-NaOH (Fig. 4D). The fluorescence intensity can be significantly reduced by UA. A similar issue arises with histidine (HIS), which significantly reduces the fluorescence intensity. To minimize the interference, Yang et al., (2022b) introduced the enzyme uricase to specifically oxidize UA and the H2O2 generated by the oxidation process could linearly enhance the chemiluminescence intensity of the luminol functionalized nanoparticles (SnO2@Luminol NPs) proposed in this study. In the presence of H2O2, the SnO2 NPs served as catalysts for luminol to generate chemiluminescence without the need for natural enzymes. Common substances such as Cys did not cause significant changes in the fluorescence intensity of the sensing system. However, the inclusion of uricase extended the overall detection time due to the required incubation reaction between UA and uricase, limiting the application of this sensor for on-site detection.

Conventional chemiluminescent reagents, such as luminol and its derivatives, often suffer from limitations in terms of stability, yield and cost. Song et al., (2022) proposed silicon quantum dots (Si QDs) as a new luminescent material that can be oxidized by potassium ferricyanide (K3(Fe(CN)6)) directly (Fig. 4E). UA, leveraging its reductive properties, was found to linearly attenuate the chemiluminescence produced by this process. However, similar to previous methods that exploit UA's reducibility, the selectivity of UA is also interfered by AA, Cys, and glucose in this study. Nevertheless, this approach exhibits promising potential for practical application due to its simplicity and cost-effectiveness.

Luminol-based chemiluminescence methods have demonstrated remarkable LODs, reaching as low as 10 pM (Saqib et al., 2018). If the challenge of interferents can be effectively addressed to enhance the selectivity of UA, the application of chemiluminescence-based sensors holds great promise for on-site and non-invasive detection, particularly in the analysis of sweat and tears. The reaction mechanism, involvement of uricase, CL probe, and synthesis methods in the mentioned studies in this section are all summarized in Table SIV.

As an amplified vibrational spectroscopic technique, SERS has emerged as an appealing analytical method for UA determination, offering advantages such as ultra-sensitivity, label-free detection, low cost, and non-invasiveness (Pucetaite et al., 2016; Villa and Poppi, 2016). When analytes are adsorbed onto a nanoscale rough metal surface, the Raman signals are significantly enhanced by several orders of magnitude (106-107 times), allowing for the extraction of fingerprint information from SERS spectra, which can be utilized for analyzing the composition and concentration of the analytes (Fig. 4B) (Hering et al., 2008; Mun et al., 2019; Xia and Li, 2021). Importantly, SERS enables direct detection of biomolecules in aqueous environments, including human urine, without the need for pre-treatment and without being affected by background signals, making it highly suitable for on-site UA detection.

Westley et al., (2017) proposed the utilization of silver colloid to enhance Raman signals for UA detection. By combining this enhancement strategy with the standard addition method (SAM) approach, they established a linear concentration range of 5–100 μM. This method proved to be applicable to real human urine samples, yielding results comparable to those obtained by HPLC, which enables absolute quantitation of UA. With the ease of urine collection, this approach presents a promising avenue for non-invasive UA detection in human body fluids. Lu et al., (2018) adopted superhydrophobic silver films with a thiol compound decorated to enhance the signal of SERS (Fig. 4F). This approach has expanded the linear range of UA to 5–1000 μM, encompassing UA concentrations found in normal serum. Wang et al., (2019a) designed a synergistic method with colorimetry and SERS for UA detection. They fabricated core-shell Ag-CDs nanocomposites with both great peroxidase-like catalytic and SERS enhancement activities, facilitating the oxidation of TMB. The generated oxTMB could be subsequently reduced to colorless TMB by UA. Additionally, SERS spectra at 1605 cm−1 were used to record changes in concentrations of UA. While the SERS method exhibited a narrower linear range, it boasted a significantly lower LOD, indicating heightened sensitivity. The combination of these two methods complemented each other, rendering this sensor with the potential for on-site applications.

In the field of SERS, increasing the activity of nano-substrates is a critical challenge and a primary requirement as it directly impacts the effectiveness of the sensor. In recent years, numerous studies have focused their efforts on addressing this issue. Alula et al., (2019) prepared silver nanostructures decorated magnetic nanoparticles. The Ag NPs coated on ZnO/Fe3O4 significantly enhanced the SERS signals, realizing direct urine testing without pretreatment. Sun et al., (2022) prepared a multilayered Ag nanoparticles SERS substrate by electroless deposition of Ag on Ti substrate. This novel substrate exhibited a substantially enhanced SERS effect, particularly for small molecule sensing such as UA. To further enhance SERS activity, Wang et al., (2022a) synthesized two-dimensional graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets loaded with Au NPs. By subjecting the nanosheets to thermal annealing treatment, the SERS activity was improved by a factor of 2.6. Consequently, the LOD of UA was achieved as low as 10−11 M, showcasing great potential for UA detection in ultra-trace biological areas like sweat and tears.

Recently, significant advancements have been made in the field of UA sensing, particularly in human body fluids and on-site detection. These developments hold great promise for portable and non-invasive UA detection. Mishra et al., (2022) developed a microfluidic-biochip device integrated with SERS. By bringing UA and plasmonic nanoparticles into close proximity, this device facilitated the aggregation of analytes, laying the foundation for the next generation of portable SERS-based UA detection. Li et al., (2022b) designed ordered inverse opal array structures composed of PDMS and Au NPs as a substrate of SERS via bottom-up self-assembly. This approach was successfully applied to the detection of UA in human tears, enabling rapid analysis within a 5-min timeframe. Moreover, it demonstrated the ability to differentiate between individuals with hyperuricemia and healthy individuals, taking the field of non-invasive UA detection a step further. Durai et al. (Durai and Badhulika, 2022) developed a wearable sweat sensor based on the TiO2@NaNbO3/PVA (SERS patch). The SERS signal was significantly amplified by the photo-induced charge transfer resonance. This wearable sensor exhibited a low LOD (1 μM) and a wide linear range (2–5000 μM) for multiple molecules, including UA. This breakthrough in on-site detection with SERS-based sensors allows for immediate and simultaneous multi-parameter detection, and its wearable nature makes it highly suitable for home healthcare and point-of-care applications. In the latest study conducted by Lu et al., (2023), a glass/pATP/Ag NPs SERS substrate was assembled for the detection of UA in sweat samples, demonstrating a linear range of 5 μM to 1 mM (Fig. 4G). This study further highlights the excellent specificity and anti-interference properties of SERS sensors, even in the absence of sample pretreatment. As a result, SERS sensors hold unique advantages for direct detection of complex biological fluid samples.

With its high sensitivity, wide response range, and excellent selectivity, SERS has emerged as an indispensable technique for the precise quantification of UA. Notably, SERS offers the distinct advantage of directly analyzing body fluids without the need for pre-treatment. Nevertheless, enhancing the reproducibility and stability of nanoparticle-based methods, along with improving the portability of SERS instruments, pose significant challenges and obstacles in the practical implementation of this approach for on-site UA detection. The SERS substrate, synthesis methods, and characteristic peak in the mentioned studies in this section are all summarized in Table SV.

SPR based sensors enable the quantification of analyte content by measuring the change in refractive index resulting from the binding of a target biomolecule (analyte) to a specific recognition site immobilized on metal and dielectric layers (Huo et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2019). Typically, SPR approaches involve measuring the shift in wavelength or the change in resonance angle (Fig. 4C). Thanks to their notable sensitivity, particularly for low-affinity interactions, label-free nature, rapid response, and high accuracy, SPR biosensors have been applied in food analysis, environmental monitoring, and medical diagnosis.

Ghodrati et al., (2022) proposed an SPR biosensor based on Ti3C2Tx MXene two-dimensional material. The sensor structure comprises an SF11 prism, BK7 glass, Ag, tungsten disulfide, and graphene. As the concentration of UA increased, the coupling activity of UA-uricase and the refractive index (RI) in the sensing medium enhanced, resulting in the linear increment in resonance angle. With an increase in the concentration of UA within the range of 595–2975 μM, there is a corresponding improvement in sensitivity from 178.25 to 187.41 deg/RIU. However, the selectivity of UA was not investigated. Jain et al., (2020) developed an LRSPR based biosensor with nanostructured silicon dioxide (SiO2) as the dielectric layer, coated with a Au layer. The resonance angle θLSPR exhibited a linear decrease as the UA concentration increased from 0.05 mM to 0.5 mM due to the decrease in RI in the vicinity of uricase/Au interface. The selectivity of UA for this sensing system was further researched to indicate that common interferers such as urea, glucose, and AA had no significant interference on UA-specific sensing. Unlike colorimetric methods that rely on measuring absorbance and LSPR peak shifts, these SPR-based sensors established a linear relationship between the resonance angle and UA concentration without the dependence of any color changes in the solution.

However, SPR-based sensors exhibit a complex structural design, and the high precision of their instruments poses limitations on their resistance to environmental interferences, thereby constraining their practical utility in on-site detection. In addition, uricase in the above studies further limits the use of these sensors in complex environments especially in low temperatures. The dielectric layer, synthesis methods, measurement methods, and involvement of uricase in the mentioned studies in this section are all summarized in Table SVI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Portable devices for UA on-site detection
ce:section.ce:para: Traditional methods for UA detection primarily relied on laboratory settings and bulky instrumentation. However, recent advancements have sought to overcome this limitation by exploring portable and on-site detection instruments, aiming to bring UA analysis beyond hospital settings and into the community and homes. In this context, considerable attention has been directed towards smartphones. The rapid progress in camera technology and the growing intelligence of smartphones have enabled them to capture high-resolution images and directly process and analyze the acquired data (Dalvand et al., 2023; Soares and Rocha, 2021; Zheng et al., 2022). Furthermore, leveraging the network communication capabilities of smartphones, several studies have established cloud servers for long-term data storage and convenient access to detection results, laying the foundation for telemedicine and internet-based diagnostics.

Wang et al., (2018b) have developed a paper-based colorimetric sensing platform for UA detection utilizing the TMB@H2O2@HRP system to facilitate color development. Chitosan has been employed to modify the paper substrate to immobilize uricase and other reagents. Following color development, the color information from the paper test was captured and processed using a smartphone. This approach has proven successful in detecting UA in serum and tear fluid, signifying significant progress in UA detection within human body fluids. In a similar vein, Zhang et al., (2022b) developed a wearable microneedle colorimetric patch employing the TMB@H2O2@Peroxidase system. When applied to the skin, this patch triggers a color development reaction in response to UA present in the interstitial fluids, resulting in a blue coloration that can be captured using a smartphone. This wearable patch offers remarkable convenience for on-site UA detection and long-term monitoring of patients with hyperuricemia. Compared to traditional blood extraction methods, the minimally invasive patch exhibits significant potential for clinical point-of-care testing, significantly reducing patient discomfort and pain. Fan et al., (2022) designed a platform based on paper-based microfluidic (Fig. 5 E). The UA sensing principle relies on the Prussian blue generation reaction, and the resulting bright blue color images can be captured by smartphone cameras. The researchers have also established a convenient point-of-care system for users to obtain test results. Moreover, this research has successfully applied UA detection to human saliva, representing a significant advancement in the field of non-invasive, on-site, and routine UA monitoring. In a related study, Duan et al., (2022) reported a photochemical sensor employing disposable test strips, a photochemical dongle, and a smartphone. This sensor effectively detects UA in fingertip blood within 2 min. Users can instantly obtain test results and subsequently seek remote consultations, eliminating the need for frequent hospital visits. This development has a positive impact on promoting Internet healthcare. The ease of operation and the nature of telemedicine have further facilitated on-site and home-based UA detection. Jain et al., (2022) have pioneered a smartphone-based fiber optic-LRSPR for UA sensing (Fig. 5D). The smartphone's complementary metal oxide semiconductor (CMOS) digital camera, equipped with built-in RGB passband filters, serves as a detector. Light from the smartphone’s LED is transmitted via optical fiber through the LSPR sensor and into the camera. This smartphone-assisted LSPR biosensor enables UA detection within a linear concentration range of 0.1 mM–1 mM, validated using real serum and urine samples. The research harnesses the advanced camera sensing technology of smartphones, significantly expanding the application potential of smartphones and the design of on-site instruments.

Studies conducted by Zhang (Zhang et al., 2022f), Gan (Gan et al., 2022), and Yuan (Yuan et al., 2022) have similarly utilized smartphones for UA detection, employing image capture and analysis techniques. The widespread use of smartphones has fueled a rising trend in portable and on-site analysis research, consequently promoting advancements in home healthcare, telemedicine, and internet-based healthcare. The integration of smartphones into UA detection methods showcases the potential for transformative advancements in healthcare accessibility and patient-centered diagnostics.

The utilization of smartphones has revolutionized the landscape of on-site UA detection, significantly enhancing its capabilities. Simultaneously, the development of specialized devices with improved accuracy and user-friendly operating procedures has opened up new possibilities in the field of on-site UA detection. These advancements demonstrate substantial potential for further advancements and applications in the realm of UA analysis.

Han et al., (2018) proposed a multiplex colorimetric bioassay platform for the detection of glucose, UA, and lactate from single-droplet samples (Fig. 5A). The platform comprises a super amphiphobic (SPO) PDMS substrate and super amphiphilic (SPI) patterns. A single-droplet sample can be precisely moved by the vacuum tip beneath the substrate due to the extremely high liquid repellency of SPO PDMS substrate. As the droplet passed through the SPI patterns, liquid droplets ranging from 0.05 to 1.4 μL in volume could be dispensed onto each SPI pattern, where they reacted with predesigned colorimetric reagents. The pink imprints on the SPI patterns of UA were generated by mixing the enzyme solution (uricase, HRP, and TRH in PBS) and the chromogenic reagent (4-AAP and DHBS). The images of the SPI patterns were acquired using a scanner and subsequently analyzed using Adobe Photoshop CS6 software to determine the UA concentration. Despite the inherent characteristics of paper-based photographic image analysis, the linear range of 0.25–4 mM has adequately fulfilled the requirement for primary screening of hyperuricemia. Although this research has not yet been applied to the detection of UA in human body fluids, it shows great promise and potential, particularly for microscopic finger-prick blood collection, on-site detection, and community screening.

In the work of Li et al., (2020b), an enzyme-free and pretreatment-free mobile healthcare (mHealth) system was developed for on-site UA detection based on colorimetric photography (Fig. 5F). The researchers utilized mesoporous Prussian blue nanoparticles (MPBs) as nanozymes and combined them with the inverse reduction process of TMB. A TMB/H2O2 mixed substrate was dropped onto the wax-printed LFPs coated with MPBs, resulting in a blue color formation. However, when a sample drop containing UA was added, the blue color faded, and this color change could be captured by a smartphone instead of a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The RGB values extracted from the photographs were automatically transformed into greyscale values using customized auto-calculation software for the determination of UA. Notably, this study achieved UA detection in whole blood without sample pretreatment, thanks to the implementation of the PEG8000 precipitation method and the design of a custom wax pattern with distinct zones for sample, detection, and background. Additionally, the researchers developed a cloud system capable of automatically reporting UA concentration by uploading the captured images. This represents a significant advancement in on-site UA detection, offering new possibilities for health monitoring in communities, hospitals, and households.

Wang et al., (2022b) have devised a centrifugal microfluidic device for whole blood on-site analysis, including UA (Fig. 5B). Similar to previous approaches, the detection module incorporates LEDs and photosensors to measure the absorbance of the sample. The plasma obtained after centrifugation is injected into the microfluidic chip, and the entire detection process is completed within 15 min. The linear range of UA (50–1100 μM) is adequate for identifying abnormal UA concentrations. This device presents a convenient solution for UA determination in blood and holds great potential for utilization in community healthcare settings and remote hospitals with limited medical resources.

In the domain of human urine analysis, our research group has designed a photodiode-based handheld detector (MHAP) for on-site UA detection (Fig. 5C) (Ma et al., 2023). UA concentration was determined by measuring the change in absorbance of the solution resulting from the reaction between UA and copper nanoparticles. The detection and calculation processes are automated within the MHAP device. This approach has been successfully applied to the detection of UA in urine, exhibiting reliable performance. Given the easy accessibility and non-invasive nature of urine samples, MHAP holds significant advantages for on-site UA detection, particularly in the context of home health monitoring.

Among body fluids, the concentration of UA in interstitial fluid exhibits the strongest correlation with blood, providing a more accurate and real-time reflection of the UA levels in the human body. He et al., (2021) have developed a colorimetric dermal tattoo biosensor based on a microneedle patch for UA sensing. By injecting a colorimetric sensing reagent containing uricase, HRP, and TMB into the skin dermal layer, varying concentrations of UA can be visualized through distinctive colors, such as orange and light green (Fig. 5G). The detection results can be directly observed by the naked eye or semi-quantitatively analyzed by capturing photos with a digital camera and extracting the hue for analysis. While this study has not yet achieved accurate quantification of UA, it is actively driving advancements in wearable technology, health management, and chronic disease monitoring in this field.

The on-site detection system utilizing smartphones as the core has revolutionized the field by enabling rapid detection and real-time analysis of signals, such as color changes, while also laying the foundation for telemedicine and internet diagnosis. This is made possible by leveraging the unique software development capabilities and internet communication features of smartphones. Additionally, the introduction of compact and handheld portable instruments has further enhanced the functionality of the detection system. These instruments facilitate various tasks, including pre-treatment of body fluids, and promote greater integration and automation, streamlining the detection process. This is particularly beneficial for users who may have limited knowledge of the underlying principles and procedures, such as older individuals living alone. The advancements in portable instruments have made on-site detection straightforward and rapid, empowering users with an accessible and user-friendly approach to monitoring their health. An essential cornerstone in the rapid advancement of on-site UA detection instruments lies in the gradual recognition of non-invasive body fluids, including sweat, urine, saliva, and tears, as highly viable alternatives to serum. These body fluids possess distinctive qualities, namely non-invasiveness, painlessness, and easy accessibility, which have significantly contributed to their escalating preference as serum substitutes. Extensive research has delved into the UA concentration in these non-invasive fluids, establishing its correlation with serum UA levels (Corey-Bloom et al., 2020; Fathallah-Shaykh and Cramer, 2014; Li et al., 2022b; Park et al., 2017; Soukup et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2022a). Moreover, studies have confirmed that the UA levels in non-invasive body fluids of patients with conditions like gout exhibit a similar trend to serum UA levels, differing from those found in healthy individuals (Pasalic et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2019d; Wu et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2020b). These findings unequivocally demonstrate that UA concentration in non-invasive body fluids can serve as a reliable indicator of serum UA levels to a certain degree. Consequently, it holds equivalent effectiveness for detecting, diagnosing, and monitoring related diseases in daily health assessments. As a result, these findings not only present theoretical feasibility but also practical value in the development of portable on-site instruments and wearable devices for UA non-invasive detection.

This review provides a systematic and detailed description of optical sensing methods and sensors for UA detection. The various types of sensors, including colorimetric sensors, fluorescent sensors, chemiluminescence-based sensors, SERS-based sensors, and SPR-based sensors, are introduced individually, highlighting their respective advantages and limitations in practical applications. Colorimetric sensors are generally easy to construct and extensively employed. They facilitate qualitative analysis of UA by directly recognizing the color variations in paper-based applications like test strips, with the naked eye. Moreover, they can be utilized in portable and miniaturized instruments for quantitative measurement of UA concentration, employing miniature spectrophotometric sensors, photodiodes, and other detection modules. Furthermore, the advanced camera modules with high resolution and the robust image analysis capabilities of smartphones can be harnessed in portable SPR devices and wearable patches, enabling UA detection. Fluorescent sensors exhibit remarkable sensitivity and low detection limits, positioning them as a popular choice in the field of UA detection. By leveraging the distinctive strong reducing properties inherent to UA, fluorescence can be effectively quenched, enabling the wide-ranging applications of fluorescent sensors in UA sensing. Notably, ratiometric fluorescent sensors emerge as a promising solution by establishing the ratio between two emission peaks, thus mitigating system errors and interference from extraneous substances. However, it is important to acknowledge that fluorescent methods typically require the use of fluorescence spectrometers and fluorescence microscopes for accurate quantitative analysis, and the availability of on-site detection equipment and systems remains somewhat restricted. Among the mentioned optical methods, chemiluminescence-based sensors stand out with their exceptional sensitivity, allowing them to detect UA at concentrations as low as 10 pM. These sensors prove particularly valuable in scenarios where UA levels are extremely low. However, one limitation of chemiluminescence-based sensors is the scarcity of on-site application equipment, which hampers their practical deployment. Additionally, they exhibit the weakest resistance to interference among the aforementioned techniques, warranting further improvement in this aspect. SERS-based sensors possess remarkable anti-interference capabilities by virtue of their ability to extract the unique fingerprint information of UA from the spectral data. Among all available optical methods, SERS-based sensors demonstrate the best selectivity for UA and enable direct analysis of untreated human body fluids. Moreover, the advancements in portable Raman spectrometers have laid the groundwork for the practical implementation of SERS-based sensors in on-site applications. SPR-based sensors offer substantial benefits in the label-free and real-time detection of UA. Nonetheless, the utilization of precise and intricate detection instruments poses limitations to their practical implementation in on-site applications. The rankings and comparisons of these five sensors in terms of sensitivity, stability, safety, simplicity, and anti-interference capability are displayed in Fig. 6 . The performances of the typical optical sensors discussed in the review have been summarized in Table I .

However, a single signal response is inevitably susceptible to various interferences, which can detrimentally impact the accuracy of detection results. Hence, the establishment of a cooperative detection mechanism that utilizes multiple signal responses, such as a colorimetric sensor array (Sun et al., 2020) or a multi-dimensional fluorescence sensing mechanism based on multiple emission peaks (Gao et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021), proves to be an effective approach for self-calibration of sensors. Moreover, alterations in the structure of nanomaterials during storage, degradation in the performance of chip surface coatings, fluctuations in environmental conditions like temperature, humidity, and air pressure, as well as the selection of diverse body fluids, all have the potential to render the existing standard curve inadequate. Consequently, in situations where significant changes occur in environmental conditions, a replacement of the target body fluid, or at predetermined intervals, it is recommended to initiate the re-establishment of the standard curve. Furthermore, the implementation of the standard addition method (SAM) (Westley et al., 2017) proves to be an effective strategy in tackling the intricate body fluid environment. These recalibration procedures guarantee the accuracy of detection results by ensuring the optimal performance of the sensors.

In the realm of designing and developing optical sensors, the incorporation of environmental and safety considerations is indispensable. On one hand, certain fluorescent sensors and chemiluminescence-based sensors utilize quantum dot materials, catalysts, and dyes that may contain heavy metals and toxic metals (Ahmad et al., 2012; Wang and Tang, 2021). Under specific conditions, these components can be released, leading to environmental pollution and potential accumulation in organisms, thereby posing risks to ecosystems. Similarly, metal nanomaterials employed in optical sensors, especially colorimetric sensors, can contribute to water and soil pollution upon discharge, causing alterations in water and soil chemistry through interactions with other substances and disrupting ecological equilibrium (Xu et al., 2022). They have the potential to accumulate within organisms, exerting adverse effects and even undergoing biomagnification in higher organisms along the food chain, thereby disturbing ecosystem balance. On the other hand, these toxic reagents and materials also present hazards to the tissues and organs of users and patients, thereby negatively impacting human health. Hence, it is crucial to prioritize the use of environmentally friendly materials, explore safer and greener synthesis methods, conduct research on the biodegradability of nanomaterials, and implement effective control measures for wastewater discharge (Li et al., 2019b; Yang and Duncan, 2021). These considerations are paramount throughout the development of novel UA optical sensors, with the aim of maximizing sustainable and environmentally friendly applications.

Moreover, the affordability of optical sensors significantly influences their extensive adoption, commercialization, and market penetration. The cost primarily stems from reagents and materials. Expensive noble metal nanoparticles, as well as biological enzymes like HRP, used in colorimetric sensors, and the synthesis and preparation of quantum dots and MOFs employed in fluorescent sensors and chemiluminescence-based sensors, contribute to high costs (Chauhan et al., 2022; Zhinzhilo and Uflyand, 2022). Another cost source lies in the instrument equipment. The considerable expense associated with large, precision instruments like spectrometers, fluorescence microscopes, Raman spectrometers, and SPR instruments significantly amplifies the challenges in applying optical sensors. The processing technology also adds to the cost, particularly in the case of SERS substrates and SPR chips that require intricate fabrication and modification (Chang, 2021; Liu et al., 2021). Therefore, in the future design and development of optical sensors, cost considerations should be incorporated without compromising sensing performance. To achieve this, it is advisable to opt for inexpensive and easily accessible nanomaterials while exploring greener and more efficient synthesis methods. Additionally, the design of on-site instruments that are compatible with the sensors, such as handheld devices based on low-cost, high-performance photodiodes and miniature spectral sensors, can be pursued. Embracing more automated and integrated processes can reduce manufacturing costs, and exploring the integration of microfluidic technology and chips can minimize instrument size and reagent usage (Wang et al., 2019b). Implementing these strategies and improvements can alleviate the cost burden associated with optical sensors, thereby further promoting their applications.

Despite the remarkable progress and significant advancements in optical sensors for UA detection, as well as the initial exploration of non-invasive and on-site applications, challenges continue to exist in terms of enzyme dependency, selectivity, automation, and wearability (Fig. 7 ). (Nangare et al., 2021; Vernerova et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020).

First of all, HRP and uricase have been extensively employed in numerous research studies (Chi et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2022b; Zhang et al., 2022d). However, it is generally observed that enzyme activities are suboptimal at room temperature. The reduced enzyme activity resulting from environmental changes can lead to unstable sensing performance, posing challenges for on-site applications. (Liang and Yan, 2019; Lin et al., 2014). Therefore, as many studies have already explored, it is necessary to replace HRP with artificially synthesized mimic enzymes to achieve enhanced stability. Furthermore, there is a need to transition from traditional indirect detection methods relying on uricase to direct detection methods. Exploring novel nanomaterials capable of directly reacting with UA is of paramount importance.

Secondly, in enzyme-free sensing systems, the quantification of UA primarily relies on its strong reducibility. However, this approach introduces interference from other substances present in body fluids, including dopamine, AA, GSH, and Cys, which also exhibit reducibility. Consequently, the selectivity of the sensing system for UA is compromised (Saqib et al., 2018; Song et al., 2022; Yue et al., 2019). Researchers can broaden their perspective beyond the reducibility of UA and explore the potential of DNA aptamers that can selectively bind to UA (Liu and Liu, 2022, 2023). This presents a promising avenue for establishing highly selective optical sensors for UA. Furthermore, in the context of human body fluid detection, the incorporation of pre-processing steps to remove specific interferents can significantly enhance the selectivity of UA.

Thirdly, there remains a deficiency in the integration and automation of the instrument's collection, pre-processing, and detection functionalities for analyzing body fluids. Limited research has been conducted on addressing the challenge of eliminating the intrinsic colors of blood and urine, which hampers the accuracy of detection outcomes. (Esteve-Turrillas et al., 2020). Although portable instruments have made substantial advancements in size reduction, the collection, pre-processing, and detection of UA in body fluids still rely on manual intervention. Achieving complete automation and integration of these functions would yield considerable benefits and hold great potential for applications in home and community healthcare settings.

Lastly, there is insufficient emphasis on wearable devices for detecting UA in sweat and interstitial fluid. Alongside microneedles and patches, smart and flexible textiles exhibit significant potential, not only in electrochemical sensors but also in the advancement of wearable optical sensors (Chen et al., 2021; Libanori et al., 2022; Zhao et al. 2021, 2022b). The shift from conventional instruments to wearable devices is an inevitable trend, and the future flourishing of wearable devices will alleviate the discomfort and burden of UA monitoring for patients, while simultaneously offering novel solutions and options for routine health monitoring in the general population.

In the future, the evolution of on-site instruments, the progress of wearable devices, and the maturation of body fluid testing technologies (including blood, urine, sweat, tears, saliva, and interstitial fluid) will pave the way for a transformation in UA detection. Moving away from traditional hospital and laboratory settings, it will transition towards Point-of-Care Testing (POCT) (Liu et al., 2019). This shift will foster a patient-centric approach to monitoring, enabling home-based healthcare and community-based disease screening models to emerge. The implications are significant, particularly for individuals with gout and other hyperuricemia conditions, as they stand to gain convenience and relief from their symptoms. Moreover, routine UA concentration monitoring among healthy individuals will be encouraged, making a noteworthy contribution to the prevention of various diseases associated with abnormal UA levels.

On another front, the advancement of smartphone capabilities and the maturity of internet technologies will solidify the establishment of cloud-based detection systems and platforms (Duan et al., 2022; Li et al., 2020b). The integration of internet-based medical care and remote consultation systems will empower patients with real-time, personalized, and confidential medical services. It may even enable the entire process of detection, diagnosis, and treatment to be seamlessly carried out from the comfort of one's own home. However, in this process, it is crucial to address the privacy and ethical concerns related to patient data. Firstly, patients have the inherent right to determine the collection, storage, and utilization of their data. The information collected by apps and related monitoring software must obtain explicit consent from patients, who should be fully informed about the purpose and usage of their data. Secondly, when it comes to data utilization and sharing, employing anonymization and de-identification techniques becomes essential to minimize the potential risks of revealing patient identities. Moreover, data collectors, such as medical institutions, health management centers, and device providers, bear an ethical responsibility to safeguard the data against unauthorized access and misuse by implementing appropriate technologies and measures that ensure its security and confidentiality. Lastly, governments and regulatory bodies should establish comprehensive laws and regulations to ensure that internet healthcare institutions and platforms adhere to legal requirements regarding privacy and data protection (Choi and Walker, 2019; Mittelstadt, 2017; Strotbaum et al., 2019). With these robust safeguards in place, patients can confidently embrace healthcare monitoring devices, including POCT for UA, as they will be built on a solid foundation and benefit from strong protection, fostering their vibrant development.
